# Cohort preference-learning benchmark

> "Chief learns your preferences" is a claim about a **population**, not one
> lucky user. The single-user reward-loop eval (`chief eval --learning`) proves
> the loop *closes*. This benchmark runs the same loop over **100 simulated
> users** and reports the **distribution**: how fast people converge, how many
> never do, and what feedback noise costs.

```bash
uv run chief eval --cohort      # writes eval/reports/cohort.md
```

## Headline (deterministic — regenerated by the command above)

- **95 / 100 users converge** to ≥95% routing agreement — median **5** rounds of
  feedback, p90 **8**.
- **Held-out interrupt F1: 0.10 → 0.87** (mean across 100 users), taught only by
  ±1 "should / shouldn't interrupt" signals — no labels, no gradient.
- The jump from an EMA-only **64%** to **95%** is the **learned-pin escalation**
  (v2, below): when nudging weights provably can't lift a wanted topic over the
  interrupt bar, the learner escalates to a hard pin. The **5** who still don't
  converge are *all* the noisiest-feedback users — the residual ceiling is
  noise-limited, not arithmetic. Nothing is swept under the rug.

## How it's set up

The cohort lives in [`eval/personas.jsonl`](../../eval/personas.jsonl), generated
deterministically from a seed by
[`eval/generate_personas.py`](../../eval/generate_personas.py) (a committed test
pins the file to the generator, so the numbers can't drift silently). Each of the
100 users has:

- **hidden per-topic interrupt preferences** — which of 12 topics they want
  interrupted, drawn from a population base-rate plus personal idiosyncrasy;
- a **scene** (chronotype) that sets their interrupt threshold — from `idle`
  (0.45) to `meeting` (0.90);
- a **feedback-noise tier** — `clean` (0.00) … `erratic` (0.40) — the fraction of
  the time they fail to correct a wrong route, the way real people are
  inconsistent.

It's a **train/eval split**, the shape a real ML benchmark uses:

| phase | data | what happens |
|---|---|---|
| **train** | the 12 topics, repeated for N rounds | Chief routes, the user emits ±1 feedback only when a route is wrong (and only `1−noise` of the time); `core.learner.Learner.record` applies one EMA weight update |
| **eval** | a **held-out** stream — 6 jittered events per topic, unseen in training | route with the learned weights vs. the uniform baseline; score interrupt precision / recall / F1 |

Everything reuses production code (`score_and_route`, `Learner.record`), so the
benchmark exercises the exact paths the running system uses.

## Three users, walked through

**`user_014` — the easy case.** `idle` scene, `clean` feedback, wants 4 topics
(including the loud `news.newsletter`). Starts at 42% agreement; **one** round of
feedback flips its two wrong topics; converges at round 1. Held-out F1 **0.38 →
0.88**. This is the anecdote the single-user eval tells — and most of the cohort
looks like it.

**`user_005` — noise slows, doesn't break.** `commuting` scene, **`erratic`**
feedback (40% of corrections dropped), wants 5 topics. Because half its nudges go
missing, it takes until round 2 to converge instead of round 1 — but it still
gets there, F1 **0.29 → 0.98**. Noise buys latency, not failure, when the
preferences are reachable.

**`user_001` — the honest ceiling.** `meeting` scene (threshold **0.90**), wants
6 topics — two of which, `oncall.page` (strength 0.40) and `build.slow` (0.38),
are simply too quiet to ever clear a 0.90 bar. It **never** reaches 95%
agreement, plateauing at 83%, F1 **0.00 → 0.64**. This isn't the learner giving
up; it's arithmetic (next section). No amount of feedback fixes it, and the
report says so.

## The ceiling, stated

EMA weights are capped at 0.5 (`core.learner.WEIGHT_MAX`). A topic whose judged
components average `s` therefore peaks at

```
score_max = Σ_dim min(0.5, s)·s = 5·s²      (for s ≤ 0.5)
```

so it can clear a scene threshold `T` **only when `s ≥ √(T/5)`**. In a `meeting`
(T = 0.90) that's `s ≥ 0.424`; a quiet 0.38-strength topic can't get there no
matter how many times the user asks. That's why 36 users cap below 100% — and it
is *correct*: preference is meant to move **borderline** decisions. Stage-1 rules
and clearly-high/clearly-low scores already handle the obvious; asking preference
to override physics would be the bug.

Without escalation this produces a clean, checkable invariant
(`tests/test_cohort.py`, `pins=False`): a user converges **if and only if** every
one of their wanted topics is reachable — `converged ∪ ceiling-capped == everyone`.

## v2 — breaking the ceiling with pins

Nudging weights forever at a topic they provably can't lift is the wrong move — so
the learner stops nudging and **escalates**. When a `should_interrupt` correction
arrives but the weight step has all but stopped (`|Δw| < 0.01` — EMA has run out of
room), `core.learner` writes a **hard per-topic pin**, and `core.brain` then routes
that topic to `interrupt` like a stage-1 rule, no judge call. It's exactly what a
chief of staff does: *"you've told me six times — I'll just always flag this."*

The effect on the same cohort, same seed:

| | EMA only (`pins=False`) | with pins (`pins=True`) |
|---|---|---|
| users converge | 64% | **95%** |
| held-out F1 | 0.10 → 0.81 | 0.10 → **0.87** |
| structurally-capped users rescued | — | **31 / 36** |

Crucially, pins only ever fire from repeated *should-interrupt*, so they only lift
**wanted** topics — precision doesn't suffer. And the escalation is honest about
its own limit: the **5** users who still don't converge are *all* `erratic`-tier —
with the noisiest feedback they never send enough consistent corrections to trip
the pin inside the training window. The ceiling that remains is **noise-limited,
not arithmetic** — the exact, checkable shape you'd want a real system to have.

## Feedback noise, by tier

| tier | noise | users | converged | F1 after |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| clean | 0.00 | 21 | 100% | 0.87 |
| light | 0.10 | 38 | 100% | 0.86 |
| noisy | 0.25 | 29 | 100% | 0.88 |
| erratic | 0.40 | 12 | 58% | 0.84 |

With pins, every tier up to `noisy` converges fully; only `erratic` (40% of
corrections dropped) lags at 58% — the residual ceiling made entirely of the
users who can't feed the loop consistently. Final routing quality barely moves
across tiers: once a preference is learned, dropped *future* corrections don't
un-learn it. That's the EMA doing its job, and the pin doing the rest.

## Files

- [`eval/generate_personas.py`](../../eval/generate_personas.py) — seeded cohort generator
- [`eval/personas.jsonl`](../../eval/personas.jsonl) — the committed 100-user dataset
- [`eval/cohort.py`](../../eval/cohort.py) — train/eval harness + report
- [`eval/reports/cohort.md`](../../eval/reports/cohort.md) — last generated report
- [`tests/test_cohort.py`](../../tests/test_cohort.py) — determinism, dataset integrity, pinned numbers, the ceiling invariant
